definition: seeming to be everywhere at once.
real_world: Reach for it when something has become so common it's hard to avoid — an app, a brand, a phrase.
example: Those QR-code menus became ubiquitous almost overnight.

definition: dealing with things sensibly and practically rather than idealistically.
real_world: Useful in work conversations to praise a realistic choice over a perfect-but-impractical one.
example: Let's be pragmatic — ship the simple version now and improve it later.

real_world: Name the quality you want (or appreciate) in a hard conversation or honest feedback.
example: I appreciated her candor about why the project slipped.

definition: very weak or flimsy; barely holding together.
real_world: A polite way to push back on a shaky argument or a stretch of a connection.
example: The link between those two events is pretty tenuous.

real_world: Ask for or praise brevity in emails, docs, and meetings.
example: Can you give me the succinct version before the call?

real_world: Steer a rambling discussion back to what actually matters.
example: The salient point is that we run out of runway by March.

definition: acting with care and foresight; sensible.
real_world: Justify a cautious decision about money, risk, or timing without sounding fearful.
example: It's prudent to keep three months of expenses saved.

definition: a subtle difference or shade of meaning.
real_world: Signal that a topic isn't black-and-white before you weigh in.
example: There's a lot of nuance here that the headline misses.

real_world: A warm, precise way to describe an outgoing person.
example: He's gregarious — he'll know half the room within ten minutes.

definition: stated or appearing to be true, but perhaps not the real reason.
real_world: Politely flag a suspected hidden motive.
example: The ostensible reason for the meeting was planning, but it felt like a review.

definition: to make a bad situation better or less painful.
real_world: Sound precise when talking about easing a problem you can't fully fix.
example: Better lighting won't end the crime but it may ameliorate it.

definition: to place side by side, usually for contrast.
real_world: Handy when comparing ideas, designs, or sets of numbers.
example: Juxtapose last year's figures with this year's and the trend jumps out.

definition: to use vague language deliberately to avoid committing.
real_world: Call out (or consciously avoid) a dodgy non-answer.
example: Stop equivocating — are we doing this or not?

definition: generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival.
real_world: Describe gracious behavior, particularly after winning or after conflict.
example: She was magnanimous in victory and thanked her opponent.

definition: overly concerned with tiny details or formal rules.
real_world: Gently flag nitpicking — often used to soften your own correction.
example: I don't want to be pedantic, but it's 'fewer,' not 'less.'

real_world: High praise for a well-built argument.
example: He made a cogent case for delaying the launch.

definition: lasting for a very short time.
real_world: Adds weight when talking about fleeting things — trends, moments, attention.
example: Internet fame is ephemeral; here today, forgotten next week.

real_world: Describe someone terse — or aim for it yourself when less is more.
example: His laconic 'fine' told me it was not, in fact, fine.

definition: very attentive to detail and cleanliness; hard to please.
real_world: Describe a meticulous person or an exacting standard.
example: She's fastidious about her code — every variable named just so.

definition: cautious; weighing all the consequences before acting.
real_world: Justify holding back judgment or information until you know more.
example: Be circumspect about what you share before the deal closes.

definition: to shock or excite someone into taking action.
real_world: Name the moment that finally spurs a person or group to move.
example: The looming deadline finally galvanized the team.

definition: based on random choice rather than reason.
real_world: Challenge a rule or number that seems made up.
example: The 200-word limit feels completely arbitrary.

real_world: Strong, specific praise for thorough work.
example: His meticulous notes saved us when the server crashed.

definition: harmless; unlikely to offend or cause trouble.
real_world: Describe something that seems harmless — sometimes deceptively so.
example: It was an innocuous question that quietly changed the whole interview.

definition: reluctant to speak about one's thoughts or feelings.
real_world: More precise than 'shy' for someone who holds back.
example: He's reticent about his past, so don't push it.

definition: real and concrete; able to be clearly seen or measured.
real_world: Demand real results over vague promises.
example: I want tangible progress by Friday, not just plans.

definition: a typical model or pattern of how something works or is thought about.
real_world: Sounds sharp when describing a fundamental shift in how things are done.
example: Remote work was a genuine paradigm shift for the company.

definition: sharp, perceptive, and good at judging situations.
real_world: A clean compliment for someone's insight.
example: That was an astute read of the room.

definition: to make something less severe or harmful.
real_world: The precise word for reducing risk or damage — common in work and finance.
example: We bought insurance to mitigate the risk.

definition: understood by only a small group with specialized knowledge.
real_world: Describe niche knowledge without sounding dismissive.
example: The talk got esoteric fast — I lost the thread after slide three.

definition: open and willing to agree or cooperate.
real_world: Test the waters before proposing something: are they amenable to it?
example: She seemed amenable to pushing the meeting to next week.

definition: friendly and easy to talk to.
example: The new manager is affable — people relax around him.

real_world: Honestly name being torn, without sounding wishy-washy.
example: I'm ambivalent about the move — great job, worse city.

real_world: Describe a conflict that ended without bad blood.
example: They reached an amicable split on the lease.

definition: something that deviates from the norm.
real_world: Flag an odd data point or one-off event precisely.
example: One bad month is an anomaly, not a trend.

definition: a lack of interest or concern.
real_world: Name disengagement in a team or yourself.
example: The low turnout came down to plain apathy.

real_world: Honest word for a genuinely hard slog.
example: Onboarding was arduous but worth it.

definition: able to express ideas clearly and well.
real_world: Compliment someone's communication, or set a goal.
example: She's remarkably articulate under pressure.

real_world: Describe an ambitious or cheeky move.
example: It was an audacious bet, but it paid off.

definition: severe or plain; without comfort or excess.
real_world: Describe a stripped-down style, budget, or person.
example: The office had an austere, no-frills feel.

definition: so ordinary as to be boring; unoriginal.
example: The speech was full of banal motivational lines.

real_world: Describe goodwill, especially from someone in power.
example: A benevolent boss who actually has your back.
I admired the brevity of her reply — two lines, all I needed.

real_world: Name blunt behavior without calling someone rude.
example: He was brusque on the call, probably just busy.
The schedule's been capricious all quarter.
Her caustic wit makes meetings entertaining and dangerous.
The board moved to censure the executive.

real_world: Describe something done quietly and on the sly.
example: They had a clandestine plan to leave the firm.
One good year made the team complacent.

real_world: Useful for drawing a line on behavior.
example: I like him, but I can't condone how he handled it.

definition: an opinion formed without firm evidence.
real_world: Label a guess as a guess in discussions.
example: That's conjecture — we don't have the numbers yet.
His apology was contrite and specific.

real_world: A classier word for a tricky dilemma.
example: Scheduling everyone is a real conundrum.

real_world: Describe a generous amount of something.
example: She took copious notes during the demo.

definition: to confirm or support with evidence.
real_world: Strong word for backing up a claim.
example: A second source corroborates the story.

definition: to desire something belonging to another.
example: Half the office covets that corner desk.
His cryptic text just said 'we'll talk.'

real_world: Describe a clunky process or object.
example: The approval process is needlessly cumbersome.

real_world: Flag a once-over that missed things.
example: A cursory glance won't catch these bugs.

definition: a scarcity or lack of something.
example: There's a dearth of senior talent in this market.

real_world: Name a fiasco with appropriate weight.
example: The launch was a complete debacle.
Let's keep some decorum in the thread.
A deft response to a hostile question.
I demurred when they suggested skipping testing.
Critics derided the idea at first.

real_world: More precise than 'sad' for deep discouragement.
example: He was despondent after the rejection.

definition: to discourage or prevent an action.
example: Higher fees deter casual buyers.
She's brilliant but diffident in big rooms.

definition: to wander from the main subject.
example: I'm digressing — back to the budget.

definition: to belittle or speak slightingly of.
example: Don't disparage the old system; it worked for years.
We're juggling disparate tools that don't talk to each other.
We'll disseminate the findings next week.
I'm dubious about those projections.
Her taste in music is wildly eclectic.
That's an egregious typo on the homepage.
An eloquent case for the rebrand.
Could you elucidate the second point?

definition: to imitate in order to match or surpass.
real_world: Talk about learning from a model.
example: I try to emulate her calm in a crisis.

real_world: Describe something hard to figure out.
example: The new hire is a bit of an enigma.
An erudite but surprisingly funny lecture.

definition: a mild term for something harsh.
real_world: Name softened language for what it is.
example: 'Letting people go' is a euphemism for layoffs.

real_world: Precise word for worsening a situation.
example: Skipping sleep will only exacerbate the stress.
Can we expedite the shipment?
Reviews extol the camera but pan the battery.
I was being facetious — don't quote me on that.

definition: a mistaken belief or flawed reasoning.
real_world: Name a logic error in a debate.
example: That's a fallacy — popularity isn't proof.
A fervent supporter of the project.
A flippant answer to a serious concern.
Meeting her there was entirely fortuitous.
Frugal habits funded the whole trip.
A furtive look at his phone gave it away.

definition: incapable of producing a useful result.
example: Arguing further was futile.

real_world: Describe someone who won't stop talking.
example: Our garrulous neighbor cornered me for an hour.

real_world: Flag smooth talk with no substance.
example: A glib reassurance that solved nothing.
The slogan felt hackneyed.
A haughty reply to a fair question.
She's an iconoclast who questions every process.

real_world: Name a quirk fondly and precisely.
example: Saving drafts in all-caps is one of his idiosyncrasies.
Poor docs impede new hires.
An impetuous reply she later regretted.
There was an implicit agreement to keep it quiet.

real_world: Soften blame for a genuine mistake.
example: An inadvertent reply-all, not malice.
The incessant notifications wrecked my focus.
An incisive question that reframed the whole debate.
I was incredulous when I saw the bill.
She was indignant about the double standard.

real_world: A pointed word for chronic laziness.
example: An indolent afternoon that ate my to-do list.
An inept rollout that confused everyone.
An ingenuous question that cut to the point.
She has an innate sense for design.

definition: harmful in a gradual, subtle way.
example: Burnout is insidious — you notice too late.
An intrepid traveler with no fixed plan.

definition: to overwhelm with a flood of things.
example: We were inundated with applications.
He's jovial even on Mondays.
A judicious use of the remaining budget.
The whole team felt lethargic after lunch.

definition: light humor in a serious context.
real_world: Name a well-timed bit of lightness.
example: A touch of levity broke the tension.

real_world: Praise clarity of thought or writing.
example: A lucid explanation of a messy topic.
Early plans should stay malleable.
A maverick who ignores the playbook — usually right.
A mercurial client — thrilled one day, furious the next.
A quick call mollified the upset customer.
He's been morose since the news.
The mundane parts of the job add up.

definition: a countless or very great number.
example: A myriad of small fixes, none urgent.

real_world: Push for specifics on a fuzzy idea.
example: The goal is still too nebulous to plan around.
A nonchalant shrug at the bad news.
The report obfuscates more than it explains.
An obsequious tone that felt fake.
An ominous silence after the question.
The reporting requirements are onerous.
The pricing is deliberately opaque.

definition: showy in a vulgar, attention-seeking way.
example: An ostentatious watch he wouldn't stop mentioning.

definition: so intense it can almost be felt.
example: The tension in the room was palpable.
More tools aren't a panacea for bad process.

real_world: Frame a puzzle that holds two truths.
example: It's a paradox: slowing down made us faster.
Parsimonious budgeting starved the project.
A penchant for last-minute changes.

definition: done with minimal effort, as a formality.
example: A perfunctory 'looks good' with no real review.

definition: harmful in a subtle, gradual way.
example: A pernicious habit of skipping breaks.
Let's stick to the pertinent details.

real_world: Describe something everywhere in a system.
example: A pervasive culture of overwork.
We placated them with a refund and an apology.
A plausible theory, but we should verify.
A poignant note from a departing colleague.
Cash flow is precarious this quarter.
A precocious coder who shipped at fifteen.
A predilection for overengineering.

definition: trying to impress with false importance.
real_world: Call out pomp without much behind it.
example: The deck was pretentious — big words, thin ideas.
Remote work is prevalent in the industry now.
A profound shift in how we work.
Side projects proliferated until nothing shipped.
A prosaic but effective solution.

definition: a state of perplexity; a dilemma.
example: I'm in a quandary over which offer to take.

definition: to suppress or put an end to.
real_world: Talk about calming or stopping something.
example: A clear plan quelled the panic.

definition: the most perfect example of a type.
example: The quintessential startup pivot story.
We built rapport before talking business.
My offer to help was rebuffed.
A recalcitrant server that refuses to behave.

definition: to respond to an action in kind.
example: They helped us, so we should reciprocate.
This paragraph is redundant — cut it.
The data refutes that assumption.

definition: able to recover quickly from difficulty.
real_world: Praise the capacity to bounce back.
example: A resilient team that learns from misses.
She stayed resolute despite the pushback.
Less rhetoric, more results.

definition: strong and able to withstand stress.
example: We need a more robust backup process.
A sardonic take on the all-hands.
Scrupulous about citing his sources.
Let's scrutinize the contract before signing.
A serene start to a chaotic week.

definition: false or fake despite seeming real.
real_world: Flag a bogus claim or correlation.
example: A spurious link between the two metrics.
Don't squander the head start.
Stoic about the setback, already planning next steps.
Stringent security requirements slowed us down.

definition: based on personal feelings, not facts.
example: 'Best' is subjective — let's define metrics.
Can you substantiate that number?
A subtle change that made a big difference.
Half these slides are superfluous.
A surreptitious glance at the answer key.
Surrounded by sycophants, he heard no bad news.
Tacit approval isn't the same as a yes.
Our taciturn lead lets the work speak.
Tedious data entry ate the morning.
Tenacious follow-up finally closed the deal.
A tentative date, pending the venue.

definition: brief and to the point, sometimes curt.
example: A terse 'noted' was all I got back.
A transient bug we couldn't reproduce.

definition: fear or anxiety about what may happen.
example: I opened the results with some trepidation.
A trite ending that undercut the talk.
Unprecedented demand crashed the site.
Stop vacillating and pick a vendor.
The team venerates the original founder.
The email was verbose — three lines would do.
A vehement objection to the timeline.
The recurring error really vexes me.
Vicarious thrills from her travel posts.
Stay vigilant about phishing emails.

definition: to clear of blame; prove right.
example: The results vindicated her early call.
A vivacious host who kept things moving.

definition: having a huge appetite (for food or activity).
example: A voracious reader — two books a week.
I'm wary of deals that good.
A whimsical design that made people smile.
A zealous advocate for accessibility.
